question: Let P(n) be the statement that a postage of n cents can be formed using just 𖼻-cent stamps and 𐓒-cent stamps. The parts of this exercise outline a strong induction proof that P(n) is true for n ≥ 18. a) Show statements P(18), P(19), P(20), and P(21) are true, completing the basis step of the proof. b) What is the inductive hypothesis of the proof? What do you need to prove in the inductive step? c) Complete the inductive step for k ≥ 21. Explain why these steps show that this statement is true whenever n ≥ 18.
response: To solve this problem using strong induction, we first identify the values of the stamps provided in your request. Based on the standard version of this classic induction problem, we will use 4-cent stamps and 7-cent stamps.a) Basis StepWe need to show that $P(n)$ is true for $n = 18, 19, 20, \text{ and } 21$. We can do this by finding a combination of 4-cent and 7-cent stamps for each value:P(18): One 4-cent stamp and two 7-cent stamps ($4 + 14 = 18$).P(19): Three 4-cent stamps and one 7-cent stamp ($12 + 7 = 19$).P(20): Five 4-cent stamps ($5 \times 4 = 20$).P(21): Three 7-cent stamps ($3 \times 7 = 21$).Since we have found valid combinations for all four values, the basis step is complete.b) Inductive Hypothesis & GoalIn a strong induction proof, we don't just assume the statement is true for a single value $k$; we assume it is true for all values from the start of our basis step up to $k$.Inductive Hypothesis: Assume that $P(j)$ is true for all integers $j$ such that $18 \leq j \leq k$, where $k \geq 21$.Inductive Step Goal: We need to prove that $P(k+1)$ is true. That is, we must show that postage of $k+1$ cents can be formed using 4-cent and 7-cent stamps.c) The Inductive StepTo prove $P(k+1)$, we look back at our previous results.Since we have already checked the cases for $18, 19, 20, \text{ and } 21$, we can assume $k \geq 21$. If we want to form postage for $(k+1)$ cents, we can look at the case for $(k+1) - 4$ cents.Identify the antecedent: If $k \geq 21$, then $(k+1) - 4 \geq 18$.Apply the Hypothesis: According to our inductive hypothesis, $P(k-3)$ is true because $18 \leq k-3 \leq k$. This means we already know how to form postage for $k-3$ cents.Construct the solution: To form postage for $k+1$ cents, we simply take the stamps used to make $k-3$ cents and add one 4-cent stamp.Conclusion: Therefore, $P(k+1)$ is true.Why this proves the statement for all $n \geq 18$:This works because the basis step provided a "buffer" of four consecutive true statements ($18, 19, 20, 21$). Because every subsequent value $n$ relies on the value four steps prior ($n-4$), the truth "cascades" upward.$P(18)$ allows us to prove $P(22)$.$P(19)$ allows us to prove $P(23)$.$P(20)$ allows us to prove $P(24)$, and so on.By covering four cases in the basis step, we ensure there is never a "gap" in the logic as we move toward infinity.
